notes.anchor: takes you for a thorough jurist
notes.note: Iure consultum: Caesar rates Trebatius highly as a iuris consultus, a legal expert. The whole letter plays on Trebatius being learned in law (sapere) yet timid in war and water; Cicero quips that even Britain would have found no one more 'learned.'

notes.anchor: Mucius and Manilius were of the same view
notes.note: Two jurists invoked as if delivering a mock legal opinion (the form a responsum took): they 'agree' that Trebatius needs a good stove against the Gallic winter. A standing joke that Trebatius's legal circle is consulted on his comfort.

notes.anchor: not exactly well supplied with cloaks
notes.note: Saga: the heavy woollen military cloak worn on campaign; 'to put on the sagum' (saga sumere) meant to go to war. Cicero teases the under-equipped Trebatius shivering in winter quarters (hiberna).

Advocationes: the business of supporting a litigant in court, the bread-and-butter of the Roman jurist and advocate. Cicero's joke is that Trebatius is bolder in the perils of the courtroom than in those of war or the sea.

Andabata: a gladiator who fought in a helmet without eye-holes, flailing blindly for the crowd's amusement. Cicero says the timid Trebatius used to be such an avid spectator that they could not even cheat him of so absurd a show.

The Aedui, a Gallic tribe formally honoured by Rome as 'brothers and kinsmen' (fratres) of the Roman people. Cicero plays on the title: one meeting with Trebatius would be worth more than enemies or even these official 'brothers' of Caesar's province.
